review_summary: The transcript is sufficient for two zero-shot CLIP questions focused on text prototypes and prompt design.

question_bank.question: How does zero-shot CLIP classify a new image without training a new classifier?
choices: It compares the image embedding to text embeddings for candidate classes and picks the nearest one
It fine-tunes the image encoder on the test set labels
It generates a caption and then runs beam search over class names
It clusters images first and then manually assigns a class to each cluster
question_bank.explanation: The text encoder can create a representation for each candidate class, and the image is classified by whichever class text vector is most similar in the shared embedding space.

question_bank.question: Why can prompts like "a photo of a dog" work better than using the single word "dog" in CLIP?
choices: Because CLIP was trained on natural language phrases, so template prompts better match its training distribution
Because CLIP can only encode complete sentences and cannot encode nouns
Because the image encoder requires the class name to contain a verb
Because prompt templates increase the dimensionality of the image embedding
question_bank.explanation: Natural-language prompt templates often better match the way the model saw text during pretraining, so the class representation becomes a better prototype for matching images.
